claim: The dominant word-initial glyph constraint (o, c, q, s, d account for most word starts) reflects a cipher rule in which a fixed 'onset marker' glyph class must precede the root â€” analogous to a mandatory null prefix â€” rather than reflecting the initial phoneme distribution of the underlying language, which would produce a flatter initial-glyph distribution matching Latin or Italian onset frequencies.
evidence_for: Five glyphs dominating word-initial positions is unusually concentrated compared to Latin (where any of ~15 consonants can be word-initial)
Glyph 'q' appears word-initially but rarely or never word-finally â€” a positional constraint inconsistent with natural phoneme distribution
Such onset constraints are consistent with a cipher rule (e.g., 'always begin encoded words with a class-marker glyph')
evidence_against: Some natural languages do have strong onset preferences; the underlying language could simply have restricted onset phonotactics
Italian word-initial vowel frequency could explain 'o' dominance if 'o' encodes a vowel class
test: Compute the entropy of the word-initial glyph distribution and compare it to the entropy of the word-internal and word-final glyph distributions. Then compare the word-initial glyph distribution to the expected initial-phoneme distribution of medieval Latin and Italian (using a reference corpus).
test_metric: Shannon entropy of positional glyph distributions (initial vs. internal vs. final); KL divergence between Voynich initial-glyph distribution and Latin/Italian initial-phoneme distributions
test_threshold: If initial-glyph entropy is >0.5 bits lower than internal-glyph entropy, onset marking is likely a cipher artifact; KL divergence >1.0 bit from both Latin and Italian would rule out direct phonemic mapping at word onset

tests_run.test: compute the entropy of the word-initial glyph distribution and compare it to the entropy of the word
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: If onset glyphs are cipher artifacts (null prefixes or class markers), stripping them before frequency analysis could significantly improve character-frequency matching to candidate languages
